_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Patrick County, VA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Formed 1791 from Henry, with Stuart as county seat. Patrick County is the most mountainous county in the Southside cluster, straddling the Blue Ridge at the Virginia-North Carolina border. The population was predominantly Scots-Irish yeoman farmers with few enslaved people — a sharp contrast to the plantation counties to the east.

current_courthouse.location: Stuart, VA — Main Street (one of the oldest courthouses in continuous use in Virginia; renovated but still serving as the county courthouse)

previous_courthouses.location: Stuart (originally called Taylorsville, then Patrick Court House) — original log courthouse
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current 1822 brick structure. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: None — no major courthouse fire or destruction
record_losses.description: Patrick County's courthouse has not experienced a catastrophic fire or destruction. Records survive continuously from the county's 1791 formation. The 1822 courthouse — still in use — has housed records for over two centuries without major incident. Patrick County's geographic isolation in the Blue Ridge foothills kept it away from major military operations during the Civil War, though the county contributed soldiers and suffered the economic hardship common to the Appalachian South. The primary research challenge is not record loss but record access — Patrick County is remote, and many records have not been microfilmed or digitized.
record_losses.narrative_impact: {ancestor_name}'s records in Patrick County almost certainly survive from 1791 onward. The challenge is access — the courthouse in Stuart sits deep in the Blue Ridge foothills, and many Patrick County records have been less thoroughly microfilmed than those of the larger Southside counties.
record_losses.source: Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county formation. Land records in Patrick County document the yeoman farm economy — smaller tracts than the plantation counties to the east.
deeds.gaps: Pre-1791 deeds for the same territory filed under Henry County.
deeds.repository: Patrick County Clerk of Court, Stuart; Library of Virginia, Richmond
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm (partial); Library of Virginia digital collections (limited)

wills_estates.completeness: Good — will books survive from county formation. Estate inventories document the modest material culture of a mountain yeoman economy — few enslaved people, small livestock herds, minimal luxury goods.
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps. Some early loose papers show wear damage.
wills_estates.repository: Patrick County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm (partial); Library of Virginia digital collections (limited)

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation.
marriages.gaps: Some early bonds incomplete. Church records (particularly Baptist and Primitive Baptist) supplement.
marriages.repository: Patrick County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous from formation. Court order books document road orders, overseers of the poor, and the governance of a mountain county with limited resources.
court_minutes.repository: Patrick County Clerk of Court; Library of Virginia

vital_records.completeness: Virginia state registration began 1912. Compliance was slow in mountain counties — many births and deaths were not registered until the 1920s or later. Midwife deliveries and home burials often went unrecorded.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records; Library of Virginia for delayed certificates

church_records.parish: No formal Anglican parish — Patrick County was settled after the Revolution and had no established church structure
church_records.completeness: Baptist church records — particularly Primitive Baptist and Missionary Baptist — are the primary church sources. The Primitive Baptists were dominant in the mountain communities. Some Methodist circuit rider records also survive. There was no Anglican/Episcopal presence of significance.
church_records.repository: Individual church archives; some records at Library of Virginia; Primitive Baptist Library, Elon, NC
church_records.online_access: Very limited digitization. Most records remain in church custody.

substitute_sources.description: Patrick County was formed from Henry in 1791. Records for the Patrick territory before 1791 are filed under Henry County.
substitute_sources.repository: Henry County Clerk of Court, Martinsville; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Patrick County families predating 1791. Henry County records cover the frontier settlement period when families first pushed into the Blue Ridge foothills.
substitute_sources.source: Pedigo, Virginia G., and Lewis G. Pedigo. History of Patrick and Henry Counties, Virginia. Roanoke: Stone Printing and Manufacturing, 1933.

substitute_sources.description: The Primitive Baptists were the dominant religious tradition in Patrick County's mountain communities. Church minutes document membership, discipline, excommunication, and the social life of isolated communities. These records are often the most detailed documentation of daily life in the mountains.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual church archives; Primitive Baptist Library, Elon, NC; some at Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Extremely valuable for mountain families. Church discipline cases document behavior, relationships, and conflicts that appear in no other record. Membership rolls serve as community censuses. Letters of dismission document migration from one congregation to another.
substitute_sources.source: Patterson, Beverly Bush. The Sound of the Dove: Singing in Appalachian Primitive Baptist Churches. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800-1940. Patrick County was not enumerated in the 1790 census (formed 1791; first enumeration 1800).
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1800 census is the first for Patrick County. The population was overwhelmingly white yeoman farmers — slave schedules show very few enslaved people compared to the plantation counties to the east.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Patrick County, VA.

substitute_sources.source_type: Revenue records and moonshine court cases
substitute_sources.description: Patrick County was a center of illicit distilling from the colonial era through Prohibition and beyond. Federal revenue agent reports, U.S. District Court cases, and county court records document the moonshine economy that was an essential part of the mountain economy.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives, Southeast Region (Atlanta); U.S. District Court records, Western District of Virginia; Patrick County Clerk of Court
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Revenue cases name individuals, describe locations (often with detailed geographic descriptions of still sites), and document an economic activity that was the primary cash-generating enterprise for many mountain families. These records are genealogically rich and often highly specific about places and people.
substitute_sources.source: Thompson, Charles D., Jr. Spirits of Just Men: Mountaineers, Liquor Bosses, and Lawmen in the Moonshine Capital of the World. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 2011.

narrative_hooks: Patrick County's courthouse in Stuart has stood since 1822, and the records inside have never burned — but they have also been less thoroughly microfilmed and digitized than the larger counties, meaning that finding {ancestor_name} may require a trip to the Blue Ridge.

The Primitive Baptists kept the most detailed records of daily life in Patrick County's mountain communities — and if {ancestor_name} was a member, the church minutes document not just membership but discipline, conflict, and the social fabric of isolated congregations.

Patrick County was moonshine country, and the federal revenue records may document {ancestor_name}'s most profitable economic activity — an activity that appears nowhere in the county's official records except in the occasional court case when {pronoun_subject} was caught.

Before 1791, Patrick was Henry County territory, and the families that settled here came down the Great Wagon Road or up the Dan River from the Piedmont — {ancestor_name}'s family entered the Blue Ridge foothills from a direction that the parent county's records may reveal.

Patrick County's estate inventories tell a different story from the plantation counties to the east — no enslaved people, few luxury goods, small livestock herds, and the tools of a mountain farm economy that measured wealth in land and labor rather than bound workers and tobacco bales.
source_references: Pedigo, Virginia G., and Lewis G. Pedigo. History of Patrick and Henry Counties, Virginia. Roanoke: Stone Printing and Manufacturing, 1933.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, 2001.
Thompson, Charles D., Jr. Spirits of Just Men: Mountaineers, Liquor Bosses, and Lawmen in the Moonshine Capital of the World. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 2011.
Patterson, Beverly Bush. The Sound of the Dove: Singing in Appalachian Primitive Baptist Churches. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 2001.
